text: Here we present a case of a patient suffering from superior mesenteric artery syndrome managed with a robotic strong procedure. Our patient was a twenty-six year old female who had a several year history of nausea, vomiting, postprandial abdominal pain, and flushing associated with an eighty pound weight loss. She had previously been managed with a PEG-J tube and initially improved with gain of weight. However, her weight loss and symptoms recurred after the PEG-J was removed. She had a CTA and upper GI series which were consistent with superior mesenteric artery syndrome. On upper GI imaging, we can see a focal narrowing of the third portion of the duodenum with diminished transit of contrast. During dynamic imaging, antiparastalsis against the narrowing was also demonstrated. CTA demonstrated an aorto-SMA angle of approximately fourteen degrees, more acute than the expected angle of twenty-five to sixty degrees, and consistent with the diagnosis of superior mesenteric artery syndrome.

The surgical team places robotic ports marked with 'R' for the procedure, with the third robotic arm serving as the camera, labeled 'C'. Two assistant ports, labeled 'A-one' and 'A-two', are also positioned.

The surgeon begins the dissection by mobilizing the ligament of Treitz from the left side, using Cotillier forceps and hook electrocautery in robotic arms. The peritoneum overlying the proximal jejunum is dissected with hook electrocautery, while the bowel is retracted inferiorly. The inferior mesenteric vein is preserved and retracted laterally to avoid injury, with the aorta visible to the left of the proximal jejunum. The dissection continues to divide thin peritoneal attachments under the SMA, and adhesions from a prior laparoscopic cholecystectomy are removed using hook electrocautery. The lateral wall of the duodenum is identified and separated from the transverse colon mesentery using electrocautery and blunt dissection.
The surgeon medializes the duodenum, revealing the inferior vena cava. The dissection plane during the COCR maneuver is maintained along the duodenum, with careful attention to avoid injury.

The surgeon mobilizes the third portion of the duodenum, dissecting cephalad along the anterior surface of the vena cava. The duodenum is retracted medially to facilitate the dissection of retroperitoneal attachments at the ligament of Treitz. A window is created posterior to the duodenum, revealing the aorta from the right side of the ligament of Treitz. The proximal jejunum is delivered through the retroperitoneum using Caudillere forceps and an assistant grasper. The dissection continues to the patient's left, enlarging the retroperitoneal window with electrocautery. A vascularized omental flap is mobilized and secured at the apex of the angle between the SMA and aorta to prevent bowel slippage.

The patient was discharged from the hospital on postoperative day two, tolerating a regular diet with complete resolution of her preoperative symptoms. She is now home, no longer having postprandial nausea and vomiting. Postoperative upper GI imaging clearly shows the duodenum and mobilized jejunum within the right upper quadrant, as well as contrast flowing freely to the rest of the small bowel. We believe that in appropriately selected patients, the STRONG procedure is an appropriate operation for symptomatic relief and that it can be done robotically in the hands of a skilled surgeon.
